coredata.dc:title: Preparation of novel fluorescent DNA bio-dots and their application for biothiols and glutathione reductase activity detection
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Qiu-Hong
Zhang, Li
Bai, Jian-Mei
Liu, Zhi-Chao
Liang, Ru-Ping
Qiu, Jian-Ding
coredata.dc:description: A new class of fluorescent bio-dots have been successfully prepared by hydrothermal treatment of poly-cytosine DNA at low temperature down to 80°C. The inter-molecule interaction of aromatic cytosine bases forms sp2 carbon-like centers as the luminescence centers or chromophores. In the presence of Ag+, the formation of C–Ag+–C base pairs results in the destroying of the luminescence centers and thus the fluorescence (FL) quenching of the bio-dots. However, with the addition of biothiols, Ag+ prefers to react with biothiols to form the Ag+–S bond, hence a restoration of FL emission can be observed. Moreover, by employing the classic glutathione reductase (GR) catalyzed enzymatic reaction, this concept can be readily applied to the selective quantification of oxidized glutathione (GSSG) as well as the activity of GR with a very robust, simple, and rapid procedure. It is worth looking forward to design DNAs with a specific function to prepare fluorescent bio-dots for simple biological applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: DNA
Bio-dots
Fluorescence
Ag+
Biothiols

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of GSSG, GR and BCNU
Fluorescence quenching of the C29-DNA bio-dots by Ag+
Fluorescence recovery of the C29-DNA bio-dots/Ag+ by biothiols

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge the supports from the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21163014 and 21105044 ), the Program for New Century Excellent Talents in University ( NCET-11-1002 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–vis absorption spectrum (blue curve) and excitation spectrum (pink curve), FL emission spectra of the C29-DNA bio-dots at different excitation wavelengths. The inset shows the photographs of the C29-DNA bio-dots under ambient light (left) and UV light (right). (B) The high-resolution O1s XPS spectrum. (C) TEM image and (D) AFM image of the C29-DNA bio-dots. The inset shows the diameter distribution and the height distribution, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(A) FL spectra of C29-DNA bio-dots in the presence of Ag+ (0.5–25μM) in 10mMpH 7.0 HEPES buffer. (B) The relationship between (I 0−I)/I 0 and Ag+ from 0.5 to 30μM.

FL spectra of C29-DNA bio-dots/Ag+ in 10mMpH 7.0 HEPES buffer treated with (A) GSH (0–30μM), (C) Cys (0–22μM), (E) Hcy (0–24μM). (B), (D) and (F) show the relationship between (I a−I)/I of C29-DNA bio-dots and biothiols concentration. I and I a are the FL intensities of C29-DNA bio-dots at 420nm with Ag+ in the absence and presence of biothiols, respectively. The concentration of Ag+ is 25μM.

(A) CD spectra of (1) DNA, (2) bio-dots, (3) bio-dots in the presence of Ag+ and (4) bio-dots in the presence of Ag+ and GSH. (B) FL decay of bio-dots as a function of time in the presence of Ag+ and GSH.

(A) FL spectra of C29-DNA bio-dots treated with 25μM Ag+, 10μM NADPH, 10μM GSSG and GR (0–40mU/mL). (B) FL intensity ratio (I 420/I 460) vs various amounts of GR. (C) FL spectra of C29-DNA bio-dots treated with 25μM Ag+, 10μM NADPH, 40mU/mL GR and GSSG (0–10μM). (D) FL intensity ratio (I 420/I 460) vs various amounts of GSSG.

Schematic illustration of the strategy for synthesis of bio-dots and their application for GSH and GR detection. Poly-cytosine DNA as precursor self-assembles to form fluorescent DNA bio-dots. The formation of C–Ag+–C base pairs result in the conformation change of bio-dots and thus the FL quenching. In the presence of GSH, the FL quenching of the bio-dots would be inhibited due to the competition for Ag+ between GSH and bio-dots.

head.ce:title: Preparation of novel fluorescent DNA bio-dots and their application for biothiols and glutathione reductase activity detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Nanchang University, Nanchang 330031, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new class of fluorescent bio-dots have been successfully prepared by hydrothermal treatment of poly-cytosine DNA at low temperature down to 80°C. The inter-molecule interaction of aromatic cytosine bases forms sp2 carbon-like centers as the luminescence centers or chromophores. In the presence of Ag+, the formation of C–Ag+–C base pairs results in the destroying of the luminescence centers and thus the fluorescence (FL) quenching of the bio-dots. However, with the addition of biothiols, Ag+ prefers to react with biothiols to form the Ag+–S bond, hence a restoration of FL emission can be observed. Moreover, by employing the classic glutathione reductase (GR) catalyzed enzymatic reaction, this concept can be readily applied to the selective quantification of oxidized glutathione (GSSG) as well as the activity of GR with a very robust, simple, and rapid procedure. It is worth looking forward to design DNAs with a specific function to prepare fluorescent bio-dots for simple biological applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Currently, fluorescent quantum dots (QDs) including semiconductor QDs (SQDs) and carbonaceous QDs (CQDs) have received great attention due to their potential applications in imaging, diagnosis and optoelectronic devices (Gill et al., 2008; Guo et al., 2013b). The high quantum yield, good photostability and large Stokes shift of QDs in comparison with organic fluorophores, also make them highly promising nanomaterials for biosensing and chemical sensing (Chen et al., 2012a; Gill et al., 2008; Sharon et al., 2010). For example, Chen et al. (2012a) designed a method to realize multiplex viruses simultaneous determination by labeling CVB3 antibody and EV71 antibody on different colored SQDs. Zhao et al. (2011) synthesized an off–on fluorescent probe of europium-adjusted CQDs for selective detection of phosphate. However, SQDs are composed of heavy metals (Pb, Te, Cd, As, etc.), which would release toxic compounds leading to environmental pollution, thus their applications are limited. CQDs are non-toxic and can be also prepared with hydrothermal approaches, however, CQDs as synthetic products usually completely lost the characteristics of the precursors, limiting their further applications. Moreover, the preparation of functionalized CQDs probe to sensitively and selectively recognize a target usually suffers from complex process (Li et al., 2013; Qu et al., 2013c). Recently, there has been a trend to synthesize fluorescent bio-dots from DNA due to their low cytotoxicity, excellent solubility, stable fluorescence (FL) and good biocompatibility (Guo et al., 2013a). It has been demonstrated that bio-dots can be formed from the cytosine-rich DNA and the stacking of the aromatic cytosine base pairs could form sp2 carbon-like centers to serve as the luminescence centers or chromophores. During the hydrothermal treatment process, amine groups and oxygen groups on different cytosine bases may react to form new O–N bonds, while retaining the PO4 1− groups on the backbone of DNA (Guo et al., 2013a). In other words, the bio-dots with comparable luminescence behavior as that of CQDs and similar structure of DNA would favor their applications in DNA-based sensors and device designs. To date, however, only one group has successfully synthesized bio-dots from salmon testes DNA (approximately 2000bp) or salmon sperm DNA (approximately 200bp) (Guo et al., 2013a) and the investigation on their biological performances and applications have not been reported. It is expected that the bio-dots derived from short deoxyribonucleic acids with special sequences would demonstrate special functions related to the precursors, and thus the controllable preparation of bio-dots and the exploration of their biological applications is of great significance.

Biological thiols, such as glutathione (GSH), cysteine (Cys) and homocystein (Hcy), as strong nucleophiles and metabolic intermediates, play a vital role in many biological processes such as keeping the suitable redox status, metabolism, detoxification, and as an active site of cofactors and enzymes (Lou et al., 2014). It's worth noting that GSH is a kind of cellular thiol as well as an essential endogenous antioxidant, which can protects the cells from oxidative stress by trapping reactive oxygen species and free radicals, it also maintains exogenous antioxidants in their reduced forms (Maity and Govindaraju, 2013). However, abnormal levels of GSH in organism could result in a large number of diseases involving aging, cancer, heart problems and other ailments (Lu, 2009; Townsend et al., 2003). Under normal conditions, glutathione mostly exists in the reduced form (GSH), it can be rapidly oxidized to glutathione disulfide (GSSG) which can be transformed to GSH by an ubiquitous flavoenzyme (glutathione reductase, GR) with the help of coenzyme II reduced tetrasodium salt (β-nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide 2′-phosphate reduced tetrasodium salt hydrate, NADPH)-mediated reduction (Winkler et al., 1994). GR is also known to play a crucial role in response to some diseases and conditions such as the genesis of anxiety and biological oxidative stress (Zhu et al., 2013). Hence, monitoring the concentration of GSH and the activity of GR in physiological media is of great importance.

The most commonly used fluorescent probes for detecting GSH are organic fluorescent probes (Lim et al., 2014; Ma et al., 2012; Yi et al., 2009). Although these organic fluorescent probes exhibit attractive sensitivity and selectivity, they possess perishing biocompatibility, poor water solubility, and complicated synthesis and purification procedures. Apparently, the development of special fluorescent probes with high water solubility, good biocompatibility, and straightforward synthesis procedures becomes very important for biothiol quantification.

Herein, new fluorescent bio-dots have been successfully synthesized by the hydrothermal treatment of DNA at fairly low temperature as low as 80°C. The stacking of aromatic cytosine bases forms the luminescence centers or chromophores and the highly hydrophilic PO4 1− groups on the backbone locate outside the sp2 carbon-like centers and wrap them to form shaped bio-dots The composition of bio-dots is very similar to that of DNA and well retains the heterocyclic rings of DNA, maintaining certain important characteristics of DNA such as the formation of metal ion-mediated base pairs (C–Ag+–C). In the presence of Ag+, the formation of C–Ag+–C base pairs results in the structural conversion and thus the FL quenching of the bio-dots due to the destroying of luminescence centers. However, upon addition of biothiols, a restoration of FL emission can be observed as a result of the competition for Ag+ between biothiols and bio-dots. Furthermore, by taking the advantage of this feature, we utilize the DNA bio-dots as fluorescent probes for the detection of biothiols and GR activity.( Scheme 1).

The DNAs 5′-(AC)29-3′, C14, C29 and C58 were purchased from Invitrogen Biotechnology Co. Ltd. (China). Oxidized glutathione (GSSG, 98%), β-nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide 2′-phosphate reduced tetrasodium salt hydrate (NADPH, 93–100%), glutathione reductase (GR) from baker's yeast and 1,3-bis(2-chloroethyl)-1-nitrosourea (BCNU, 98%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). Reduced glutathione (GSH, 99%) was provided by J&K Scientific Ltd. (China). Silver nitrate (AgNO3, 99.8%) was obtained from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (China). All the reagents were of analytical grade and were used without further purification. Ultrapure water (18.2MΩ, Milli-Q Integral) was used in all experiments. Human serum samples were obtained from Jiangxi Provincial People's Hospital.

The morphology of bio-dots was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) using a JEM-2010 TEM facility (JEOL, Japan). Atomic force microscope (AFM) images were obtained using a Bruker MultiMode-8 AFM/SPM (Bruker, Germany) with the ScanAsyst mode under ambient conditions. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) characterizations were performed on a VG Multilab 2000X instrument (Thermal Electron, USA). Fluorescence (FL) spectra and light scattering (LS) spectra were recorded using a F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). The UV–vis absorption spectrum was obtained with an UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Circular dichroism (CD) spectra of the DNA and bio-dots solution were collected with a Mos-450 spectrophotometer (Bio-logic, France) in HEPES buffer (10mM, pH 7.0) at room temperature.

The C29-DNA bio-dots were prepared from C29-DNA by a hydrothermal approach. In brief, C29 DNA was dissolved in ultrapure water with a concentration of 9.32μM. Then the solution was put into a poly-(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave and heated at 80°C for 12h. As a result, uniform C29-DNA bio-dots were obtained. (AC)29-DNA bio-dots, C14-DNA bio-dots and C58-DNA bio-dots were prepared from (AC)29-DNA, C14-DNA and C58-DNA with the same procedure as for the preparation of C29-DNA bio-dots.

To 50μL of bio-dots solution were added 50μL of 10mM pH 7.0 HEPES buffer solution and different concentrations of Ag+ (from 0 to 30μM). The mixture was diluted with ultrapure water to a volume of 200μL. After incubation at 37°C for 5min, the FL spectra of the resulting solution were recorded at λ ex=290nm. The FL intensity was measured at 420nm.

Different amounts of GSH solution (from 0 to 30μM) were added to 25μL of 0.2mM Ag+ (25μM final concentration) diluted with 50μL of 10mM pH 7.0 HEPES buffer solution, followed by the addition of 50μL of bio-dots. The mixed solution was diluted with ultrapure water to a volume of 200μL. After incubation at 37°C for 5min, the FL spectra of the resulting solution were recorded at λ ex=290nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of GSSG, GR and BCNU
ce:section.ce:para: For GSSG detection, 20μL of 0.4U/mL GR (40mU/mL final concentration) was mixed with 10μL of 0.2mM NADPH (10μM final concentration) and varying concentrations of GSSG (from 0 to 10μM) in 50μL of 10mM pH 7.0 HEPES buffer at 37°C for 5min. Afterwards, 25μL of 0.2mM Ag+ (25μM final concentration) and 50μL of bio-dots were added to the mixture, which was then diluted with ultrapure water to a volume of 200μL. After incubation at 37°C for 5min, the FL emission spectra were measured at λ ex=290nm.

For GR detection, 20μL of 0.1mM GSSG (10μM final concentration) was incubated with 10μL of 0.2mM NADPH (10μM final concentration) and varying concentrations of GR (from 0 to 40mU/mL) in 50μL of 10mMpH 7.0 HEPES buffer at 37°C for 5min. Afterwards, 25μL of 0.2mM Ag+ (25μM final concentration) and 50μL of bio-dots were added to the mixture, which was then diluted with ultrapure water to a volume of 200μL. After incubation at 37°C for 5min, the FL emission spectra were collected at λ ex=290nm.

For GR inhibitor (BCNU), 20μL of 0.4U/mL GR (40mU/mL final concentration) was pre-incubated with various concentrations of BCNU (from 0 to 0.347mM) in 50μL of 10mM pH 7.0 HEPES buffer at ambient temperature for 10min, then 20μL of 0.1mM GSSG (10μM final concentration) and 10μL of 0.2mM NADPH (10μM final concentration) were added to start the reaction. After incubation at 37°C for 5min, 25μL of 0.2mM Ag+ (25μM final concentration) and 50μL of bio-dots were added to the mixture, which was then diluted with ultrapure water to a volume of 200μL. After incubation at 37°C for 5min, the FL emission spectra were measured at λ ex=290nm.

To investigate the optical properties of the bio-dots, UV–vis absorption and FL behavior were investigated. As shown in Fig. 1A, the bio-dots with a molar absorption coefficient of 2.2×105 Lmol− 1 cm− 1 show a strong peak at 280nm which is similar to the characteristic absorption peak of DNA (260nm) with a distinct redshift of 20nm. The excitation spectrum of the bio-dots exhibits a peak at 290nm, and the maximum emission locates at 420nm with 290nm excitation. It is worth noting that there is no perceptible shift of the maximum emission peak at excitation range from 260 to 320nm, and this excitation-independent emission behavior implies that both the size and the surface state of the sp2 carbon-like centers in the bio-dots should be uniform (Liu et al., 2014; Qu et al., 2013a; Zhuo et al., 2012), which is completely different from other fluorescent CQDs and previous bio-dots synthesized by salmon testes DNA or salmon sperm DNA (Guo et al., 2013a; Qu et al., 2013b; Zhao et al., 2011). Photographs of the bio-dots under room-light and UV irradiation are shown as insets in Fig. 1A. A blue color was observed under UV irradiation, indicating the formation of highly fluorescent bio-dots. In order to certify that the hydrothermal treatment of DNA is necessary to obtain fluorescent bio-dots, we compared the FL intensity and the FL quantum yield (QY) of the DNAs by simple incubation at 80°C for 12h with those by hydrothermal treating (Fig. S1). It demonstrated that the FL intensity as well as the QY of the products incubated at 80°C for 12h (the QY was about 0.5%) were much lower than those of the hydrothermally treated bio-dots (the QY was about 1.1%). These results prove that the hydrothermal treatment is essential for the generation of O–N bonds to form the luminescence centers and finally the fluorescent bio-dots, while the simple incubation and annealing at 80°C for 12h is unable to generate such fluorescent bio-dots. We further utilized XPS characterization to explore the composition and configuration of C29-DNA bio-dots. The full range XPS analysis (Fig. S2A) of the resultant C29-DNA bio-dots clearly shows four typical peaks of P2p (133.5eV), C1s (284.6eV), N1s (400.0eV) and O1s (532.1eV), evidencing that the composition of the bio-dots is close to that of DNA (Majumder et al., 2009). A high resolution spectrum of O1s (Fig. 1B) exhibits four peaks at 530.8eV (PO4 1−), 532eV (O=C), 532.6eV (O–N) and 533.2eV (O–C), respectively. As we all know that DNA contains O–C, PO4 1− and O=C while lacking O–N (Majumder et al., 2009), hence, it is very likely that the O–N band of bio-dots comes from the interaction between base pairs of DNA during the preparation (Olivares et al., 2006), whereafter, to form a special molecular structure as luminescence centers accounting for the photoluminescence of bio-dots. Besides, the P2p, C1s and N1s peaks are consistent with that of DNA (Fig. S2B–D) (Majumder et al., 2009), indicating that the formed bio-dots retain the heterocyclic rings of DNA, which is the basis for the interaction between bio-dots and Ag+ via the formation of unusual C–Ag+–C base pairs. In order to further certify the formation of bio-dots, we employed TEM and AFM to characterize the bio-dots. As can be seen from Fig. 1C, the C29-DNAbio-dots are fairly uniform spheres well separated from each otherwith the averaging diameter of about 16nm (Fig. 1C) and a typical topographic height of about 0.8nm (Fig. 1D). These results unambiguously show that the DNA bio-dots with unique optical properties and uniform morphologies have been successfully prepared.

In order to further explore the unique FL properties of the bio-dots synthesized from cytosine-rich DNA, we utilized four different sequences of DNA ((AC)29, C14, C29 and C58) to synthesize different bio-dots and their corresponding FL spectra were recorded (Fig. S3A). The results show that the optimum excitation/emission wavelength of the four kinds of bio-dots are 300nm/390nm, 290nm/420nm, 290nm/420nm and 290nm/420nm, respectively. The FL spectra of the bio-dots display DNA sequence-dependent property, where the consecutive cytosines are favorable to the synthesis of highly fluorescent bio-dots. The FL intensity enhanced with the increasing number of cytosine bases. To some extent, consecutive cytosines might be beneficial to form luminescence centers. More cytosines will result in more luminescence centers, and hence the higher FL intensity. This is consistent with previously reported results (Guo et al., 2013a). Intensive investigation on the relationship between the optical properties of the bio-dots and the base number/base arrangement of DNA is in progress.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching of the C29-DNA bio-dots by Ag+
ce:section.ce:para: In order to verify that the bio-dots maintain the basic biological properties of DNA, we investigated the interaction between bio-dots and Ag+. In the absence of Ag+, the bio-dots maintain shaped structure which can be easily radiative recombination under certain excitation. However, in the presence of Ag+, the shaped structure of bio-dots will be destroyed through the formation of the stable C–Ag+–C base pairs (Lin et al., 2011; Zhang et al., 2015), therefore the FL of the bio-dots is quenched as a result of the destroying of the luminescence centers. The FL intensity of C29-DNA bio-dots reduces as the concentration of Ag+ increases ( Fig. 2A). About 80% FL is quenched by addition of 25μM Ag+. Fig. 2B shows the quenching efficiency ((I 0−I)/I 0) versus Ag+ concentration which has a good linear fit with a correlation coefficient of 0.996. Subsequently, we further investigated the interaction between the other three kinds of bio-dots and Ag+ based on C–Ag+–C coordination chemistry, the C29- and C58-DNA bio-dots demonstrate more sensitive response to Ag+ than other two bio-dots (Fig. S3B). The high dependence of quenching efficiency on DNA sequences may be ascribed to cytosine base numbers as well as the cytosines base arrangement, where long sequences with consecutive cytosines would favor the Ag+ binding. Notably, other monvalent-ions (Au, K, Na, etc.) cannot induce the FL quenching of bio-dots since they cannot interact with the cytosine of the bio-dots to form base pairs (Lin and Tseng, 2009).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence recovery of the C29-DNA bio-dots/Ag+ by biothiols
ce:section.ce:para: As we mentioned above, the interaction between the bio-dots and Ag+ results in the FL quenching of the bio-dots. It has been reported that thiol-contained compounds could react with Ag+ as a result of the strong Ag–S bond (Li et al., 2009), thus, it is reasonable to expect that the presence of biothiols could recover the FL of bio-dots due to the competition for Ag+ between thiols and bio-dots. As demonstrated in Fig. 3A, upon addition of the pre-incubated GSH/Ag+ to the C29-DNA bio-dots solution, the FL of C29-DNA bio-dots recovers and the FL intensity enhances gradually with the increase of GSH concentration. The reason can be inferred that in the presence of GSH, the Ag–S bond could be formed due to the strong binding preference between the thiol groups and Ag+ (Chen et al., 2012b; Li et al., 2009), resulting in the decreased concentration of Ag+ conjugated on bio-dots. Thus, the restoration of bio-dots FL can be observed. The FL enhancement at 420nm is plotted as a function of GSH concentration, showing a good linear relationship between recovery efficiency ((I a−I)/I) and GSH concentrations over the range of 0.2–30μM (Fig. 3B), the limit of detection (LOD) for analyzing GSH corresponds to 0.0625μM (3σ), which is lower than or comparable to those of previously reported detection strategies (Table S1) (Garai-Ibabe et al., 2013; Mu et al., 2014; Niu et al., 2012). Similarly, the method could be also applied to assay other biothiols such as Cys and Hcy. The FL intensity increases upon addition of Cys (Fig. 3C) and Hcy (Fig. 3E), and their linear ranges are 0.5–22μM (Fig. 3D) and 0.5–24μM (Fig. 3F), with the LOD of 0.083μM and 0.091μM, respectively, which are much lower than the regular concentration of biothiols in human fluids (0.1–10mM of GSH in cell, 50–300μM of Cys in plasma, and 5–15μM of Hcy in plasma) (Chen et al., 2012b). With the purpose of determining whether our system is specific for thiols, the influences of a variety of amino acids were tested. As exhibited in Fig. S4, only GSH, Cys and Hcy can induce a high FL recovery, naturally occurring amino acids do not cause an evident FL enhancement under the same conditions. Cys is the most abundant thiol compound in human plasma (Huang et al., 2011; Ma et al., 2012), therefore, we used Cys as the standard to examine the unknown concentrations of thiol compounds in spiked human serum samples by a standard addition method and the results were shown in Table S2. The recovery values were in the range of 95–105%, indicating that our system can serve as a simple and sensitive approach for biothiols sensing in practical samples.

For the purpose of obtaining the optimal experimental conditions for the proposed method, we first studied the effect of response time on the FL quenching efficiency by Ag+ and on the FL recovery efficiency by GSH. When 25μM Ag+ is added into the C29-DNA bio-dots solution in 10mMpH 7.0 HEPES buffer, the quenching efficiency ((I 0−I)/I 0) increases during the early stage of the reaction and reaches a plateau after 5min (Fig. S5A, red line). In the presence of GSH, the recovery efficiency ((I a−I)/I) quickly reaches a plateau with the increasing response time of Ag+ and GSH (Fig. S5A, black line). We also investigated the pH effect on the FL quenching efficiency by Ag+ as well as on the FL recovery efficiency by GSH. The solution pH demonstrates a negligible effect on the FL quenching efficiency ((I 0−I)/I 0) by Ag+ (Fig. S5B, red line), while a relatively great effect on the FL recovery efficiency ((I a−I)/I) by GSH (Fig. S5B, black line). In order to obtain a lower LOD and to facilitate the potential application in real biological samples, the buffer of pH 7.0 is used in this experiment.

According to the experimental phenomena, we can infer that the FL quenching of the C29-DNA bio-dots is due to the conformational change rather than the aggregation of bio-dots. Light scattering (LS), AFM and CD characterizations were carried out to confirm the suggested mechanism presented in Scheme 1. As we know, LS is an extremely selective and sensitive method for monitoring intermolecular assembly, which can be used to monitor the distance change as well as the particle size evolution (Liu et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 2013a; Zhang et al., 2008). Generally speaking, the LS intensity increases with decreasing distance between assembled nanoparticles (Song et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2013). As shown in Fig. S6A, there is no noticeable change of the LS intensity of the bio-dots treated with various amounts of Ag+, suggesting that the aggregation of the bio-dots does not occur and the distance between bio-dots does not have significant change. Subsequently, we investigated the topographic morphology of the C29-DNA bio-dots treated with Ag+. In the presence of Ag+ (Fig. S6B), the topographic height of bio-dots is about 0.5nm while retaining monodispersed state, demonstrating that the bio-dots display slightly unfolded state with respect to that in the absence of Ag+. To further assess the performance and conformance of the experimental results, CD measurements ( Fig. 4A) were also used to investigate the proposed mechanism. The CD spectrum of bio-dots (curve 2) shows a positive band at 285nm, which is less positive than the free DNA (curve 1), suggesting that the formation of bio-dots has no effect on its basic structure of DNA but a more tightly folded DNA structure is formed as a result of the hydrothermal reaction (Chen et al., 2011). However, in the presence of Ag+ (curve 3), a new negative peak at 268nm appears, and the positive band at 285nm decreases and redshifts to 297nm, indicating the formation of C–Ag+–C complexes (Chen et al., 2011; Petty et al., 2004; Zhang et al., 2013b). Thus, the probable reason for the FL quenching may be that the Ag+ reacts with nitrogen atoms in cytosines to form Ag–N bond (Zheng et al., 2014), which destroys the luminescence centers of the bio-dots. Moreover, in the presence of GSH, the CD spectrum (curve 4) of the bio-dots/Ag+/GSH system is similar to that of the bio-dots (curve 2), indicating that Ag+ prefers to bind with GSH to prevent the conformation change of the bio-dots. In order to verify the FL recovery of bio-dots is due to the reaction between Ag+ and GSH that maintains the luminescence centers of bio-dots, we further performed the following control experiment to evaluate the effect of GSH alone on the optical property of bio-dots (Fig. S6C). The results reveal that the FL intensity of C29-DNA bio-dots shows a negligible change with the addition of different concentrations of GSH, further confirming that the recovered FL only results from the formation of Ag–S bond between Ag+ and GSH. In order to confirm the nature of the quenching, fluorescence lifetime measurements were performed. The fluorescence decay curves are shown in Fig. 4B, the lifetime of C29-DNA bio-dots did not obviously change with the addition of Ag+ and GSH. Therefore, the quenching of the C29-DNA bio-dots by Ag+ obeyed a simple static quenching mechanism-i.e., the Ag+ coordinated with bio-dots and destructed the luminescence centers. In other words, the FL quenching could be attributed to the disappearance of bright species with a remaining population of bio-dots of comparable brightness.

By introducing GR and the coenzyme NADPH, GSSG can be transformed to GSH through a catalytic reduction, and the latter could efficiently restore the FL of bio-dots by combining with Ag+. As exhibited in Fig. 5A, when GSSH and coenzyme NADPH exist but without GR, the FL of bio-dots are quenched by Ag+ and only the FL of NADPH (λ max=460nm) can be observed (Davidson and Flynn, 1979; Sebastian et al., 2004). However, in the presence of GR, the FL intensity of bio-dots enhances gradually along with increasing the GR concentration. Meanwhile the FL intensity of NADPH decreases gradually, demonstrating that an efficient GR-catalyzed oxidization of NADPH occurs. As a consequence, the GR-catalyzed reduction of GSSG can be readily monitored, and the enzyme itself can be assayed quantitatively. As shown in Fig. 5B, the ratio of I 420/I 460 displays linear relation with the GR concentrations in the range of 4–40 mU/mL with the LOD of 0.5 mU/mL.

In view of the fact that the level of GSH/GSSG ratio is a pivotal indicator in monitoring the cells' overall health (McMahon and Gunnlaugsson, 2012; Rahman et al., 2007), we also studied the response of this system to different concentrations of GSSG (Fig. 5C). When GR and coenzyme NADPH except for GSSG exist, the fluorescent bio-dots are quenched by Ag+ and only the FL of NADPH (λ max=460nm) can be observed. As the concentration of GSSG increases, the emission intensity of bio-dots increases. Meanwhile, the emission intensity of NADPH decreases. Results in Fig. 5D demonstrate that the ratio of I 420/I 460 increases along with the GSSG concentration with a linear range of 2–10μM.

Some control experiments were then carried out to verify the suggested mechanism of the enzymatic analytical system presented in Scheme 1. As shown in Fig. S7, none of these three species (NADPH, GSSG and GR) display a remarkable influence on the FL of bio-dots, neither in alone nor in a combination of the two species. In contrast, a great enhancement in the FL signal is observed when all components (NADPH, GSSG, GR, Ag+ and bio-dots) of the protocol are presented in the mixture. Therefore, the observed FL recovery of the bio-dots/Ag+ can be only due to the generation of GSH combining with Ag+ during the GR-catalyzed reduction of GSSG.

The inhibition process can be also monitored by the present effective GR activity protocol. In our study, the inhibition effect of the most commonly used irreversible GR inhibitor 1,3-bis(2-chloroethyl)-1-nitrosourea (BCNU) was examined (Karplus et al., 1988). As shown in Fig. S8, the turn-on FL response is apparently prevented in the presence of BCNU, the FL intensity ratio (I 420/I 460) reduces gradually with the increase of BCNU concentration, denoting that the sensing method can be used in screening potential drugs based on the inhibition of GR.

In summary, we have successfully synthesized fluorescent bio-dots by hydrothermal treatment of C-rich DNA at low temperature down to 80°C. Besides being inexpensive, green and sustainable, such a low temperature of hydrothermal process has the advantage to retain the activity of DNA to bind with special metal ions (C–Ag+–C), and a variety of characterizations have proved this amazing phenomenon. Furthermore, we developed a FL turn-on system for the detection of biothiols based on the Ag+-mediated FL quenching of bio-dots as well as the well-known reaction between thiols and Ag+. In addition, by employing the classic GR-catalyzed enzymatic reaction, GR, GSSG sensing and inhibitor screening can be also facilely realized.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the supports from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21163014 and 21105044), the Program for New Century Excellent Talents in University (NCET-11-1002).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi: 10.1016/j.bios.2015.07.018.
